--

The Problem
Recognising patterns in mood and physical pain can be difficult when relying solely on memory. Many people often experience fluctuations in their wellbeing that may go unnoticed without a consistent method of recording experiences over time. This project explores how a web application can assist users in tracking and visualising this information to support self-reflection.
Research into self-tracking technologies suggests that recording personal health information can improve self-awareness and support health and wellbeing by helping users identify patterns in their own data.
Tools like Daylio, Moodfit and eMoods exist to solve similar problems, and said existing applications demonstrate that mood tracking is an established approach to personal wellbeing, although implementations vary in complexity and intended audience.
To address the problem, The Village was developed as a full-stack web application using the MERN stack. The app allows users to record mood and pain-related experiences, store this information securely, and visualise the resulting data through graphs. The aim of the project was to provide users with a centralised platform for monitoring their wellbeing while also exploring the technical and ethical considerations involved in developing applications that handle personal information.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Technical Considerations
As the core frontend tech of MERN, React was used because it allowed the application to be broken into reusable components. This made it easier to build pages such as Mood, Pain and Events without duplicating large amounts of code. Coupling with MaterialUI, it assisted with development of features such as forms, dashboards, navigation menus, and data visualisations while keeping the code organised.
Node.js was used as the runtime environment for the backend application. It enables JavaScript to be used on both the frontend and backend, reducing the need to switch between programming languages and simplifying development across the entire project.
Express.js was used as the backend framework because it provides a lightweight and flexible way to create APIs and manage HTTP requests. It was used to handle user authentication, process requests from the frontend, and interact with the database.
And MongoDB ofcourse, because the application’s data naturally fits a document structure. Mood entries, pain entries and event markers all contain different types of information, making a document database a practical choice.
We took advantage of Mongoose as an Object Data Modelling (ODM) library for MongoDB. It provided schema validation, data modelling, and simplified database operations, helping ensure that data entered into the application met expected formats and constraints.
JWT authentication was implemented to secure access to user accounts and protected routes. Once authenticated, users receive a token that verifies their identity when making requests to the backend, reducing the need to repeatedly send login credentials.
Material UI was used to create a consistent and responsive user interface. The library has pre-built components such as buttons, forms, dialogs, and navigation elements, allowing development time to be focused on application functionality rather than building interface components from scratch.
To summarise, the MERN stack was selected because it provides a complete JavaScript-based solution for full-stack web development (MongoDB, 2026). React delivered a dynamic user interface, Node.js and Express.js provided the backend infrastructure and API functionality, while MongoDB and Mongoose handled data storage and validation. Supporting technologies such as JWT and Material UI enhanced security, usability, and data visualisation. Together, these technologies supplied the development of a scalable application capable of collecting, storing, and presenting wellbeing data in a meaningful way.
Ethical Considerations
As The Village allows users to record information about their mood, pain levels, and personal experiences, privacy and data security were important considerations throughout development. While the application is not intended to replace professional medical advice or act as a healthcare service, it does store information that many users may consider sensitive.
To help protect user data, authentication was implemented using JSON Web Tokens (JWT), ensuring that users can only access their own information once authenticated. Passwords are also securely processed before being stored in the database rather than being stored in plain text. Additional security measures were implemented through packages such as Helmet, which helps protect against common web vulnerabilities by setting secure HTTP headers, and CORS, which restricts how resources can be accessed across different origins.
Another ethical consideration was ensuring that the application presents information in a clear and responsible manner. The visualisations shown by The Village are intended to help users identify potential patterns in their wellbeing data, however they should not be interpreted as medical diagnoses or professional health advice. Users should consult qualified healthcare professionals when making decisions about their health or treatment.
By considering privacy, security, and responsible presentation of information, the project aims to provide a useful wellbeing tracking tool while respecting the sensitive nature of the data being recorded.
Implementing The Village
The Village was developed as a full-stack MERN application consisting of a React frontend, an Express.js and Node.js backend, and a MongoDB database. The project was designed to provide users with a simple and accessible way to record wellbeing-related information and visualise changes over time. The application supports mood tracking, pain tracking, event logging, journalling, user authentication, and graphical reporting features.
The backend was implemented using Express.js and MongoDB. RESTful API endpoints were created to support CRUD (Create, Read, Update and Delete) operations for users, mood entries, pain entries, and events. Mongoose was used to model application data and enforce validation rules, while JWT authentication was implemented to secure protected routes and ensure users could only access their own information. Additional security measures were provided through Helmet, secure password hashing, and environment variables for sensitive configuration data.
The frontend was developed using React and Vite. React components were used to create reusable user interface elements such as navigation menus, authentication forms, and data entry pages. React Router was used to manage navigation throughout the application, while Material UI provided a consistent design system and responsive components. The application was designed to function across both desktop and mobile devices through the use of responsive layouts and navigation patterns.
The key feature of the application is the ability for users to record mood, pain, and event entries with optional notes. These records are stored within MongoDB and retrieved through the backend API when required. Users can then view their historical data through graphical reports, making it easier to identify patterns and trends that may otherwise be difficult to recognise through memory alone. Event markers provide additional context by allowing users to record significant life events that may influence changes in mood or pain levels.
To improve maintainability, both the frontend and backend applications were organised using a modular structure. Components, pages, services, routes, middleware, models, and utilities were separated into dedicated directories. This approach reduced code duplication, improved readability, and made it easier to develop, test, and troubleshoot individual features throughout the project lifecycle.
Reflection
Prior to developing The Village, I had previously created a MERN application which was a movie reviews platform. While this project gave me experience with React, Express.js, Node.js and MongoDB, it was developed using standards that were common around 2021 from a book. During that project, one of the main challenges was working with modern packages and frameworks alongside older development approaches. Several Mongoose features and implementation methods had since been deprecated, requiring additional research and workarounds to complete the application.
The development of The Village helped to build upon these existing skills while working with a more modern technology stack. Throughout the project, I gained a stronger understanding of API design and implementation through the creation of RESTful endpoints for users, moods, pain entries and events. I also improved my understanding of database design by creating Mongoose schemas that enforced validation rules and maintained consistency across application data.
One of the more significant areas of growth was application security. The previous movie reviews project had only basic authentication and authorisation functionality, whereas The Village required a more comprehensive approach due to the sensitive nature of wellbeing-related information. Through implementing JWT authentication, middleware-based route protection, password hashing and salting, and secure environment variable management, I developed a greater appreciation for the importance of security within modern web applications.
The project also pushed the importance of planning and maintainable code structure. Applying separation of concerns, I split the functionality into controllers, middleware, models, components, pages and services, and the application became easier to extend and troubleshoot as development progressed. The modular approach improved code readability and reduced the likelihood of introducing issues when implementing new features.
If the project were to continue beyond its current scope, additional functionality could be put in place, including better reporting tools, data export capabilities, notification systems and improved accessibility features. While the core features of the application were completed successfully, time constraints limited the implementation of several potential enhancements. Future development would also benefit from additional user testing to evaluate usability and identify areas for further improvement. We unfortunately didn’t have any user testing when the application was completed.
Overall, The Village helped my full-stack development skills, improve my understanding of app security, and gain practical experience designing and implementing a larger-scale MERN application than I had previously undertaken.
References
Self-tracking technologies have become increasingly common in healthcare and personal wellbeing, enabling users to record and monitor information over time. A systematic review by Feng et al. (2021) found that self-tracking is widely used to support health and wellbeing through the collection and analysis of personal data.
